text: The best analogy I've ever heard for why sales process breaks down didn't come from a business book. It came from my high school driver's ed instructor. He used to say: 'It takes 2,534 bolts to assemble a Ferrari and one nut to scatter it all over the road.' I think about it every time I see a GTM strategy fall apart in the field. In RevOps we build perfect go-to-market engines. Hyper-tuned processes, airtight methodologies, beautifully designed CRM workflows. Then we ask reps to maintain every bolt in between sales calls. That's the mistake. Reps are wired to build relationships, solve problems, and close deals. They're not wired to manage process.

I once spent a month looking for a rep who would run through walls. I screened hard for it. Are you an out-of-the-box thinker? What's the furthest you've gone to get a deal done? I finally found the person. Hired them on the spot. Day one, I put them in training where they spent the day walking through every field they needed to fill out in CRM and every step of the process they were supposed to follow. I remember sitting there thinking — I just spent a month finding someone with great instincts. Someone who would do whatever it takes to get the deal done, and the first thing I did was slow them down with a rulebook. That's the tension sitting inside most sales orgs. We hire for tenacity and then ask those same people to spend their day maintaining process. Agents fix that. They handle the process so the rep never has to.

Every revenue org has the same $5 tasks eating into rep capacity. CRM updates. Meeting prep. Call summaries. Follow-up logging. Internal reporting. None of it closes deals. All of it takes time that should be going toward $5,000 tasks: building relationships, running discovery, and moving opportunities forward. These $5 tasks are important, but they don't have to be done by reps. At Terret our agents handle $5 tasks in the background automatically, consistently, without the rep ever having to think about them. With AI doing the heavy lifting, sales productivity goes through the roof, and you find out what your team is actually capable of.

Elon Musk has a secret your sales team is missing. When one Tesla hits a pothole in Palo Alto, the entire fleet learns how to avoid it by morning. One car learns, and the neural network evolves. Your revenue org doesn't work that way. Let's say 'Mike' is your superstar. He just found a killer rebuttal to a new competitor and closed a $200k deal. Your other 15 reps? They're still losing to that same objection. There's no (legal) way to clone Mike, so your revenue org is a fleet of cars crashing into the same obstacles over and over. Traditional sales enablement doesn't fix this. It's a graveyard of 50-slide decks that everyone forgets by Tuesday. What your revenue org actually needs is a central nervous system. One that takes what Mike knows and automatically spreads it to everyone. That's exactly what Terret Nexus does. We analyze your top performers to see exactly why they win, turn those wins into a repeatable system, and before a rep jumps on their next call, Terret Nexus hands them the exact playbook your best closer used to win yesterday. Suddenly, the middle of your leaderboard performs like the top. When one of your reps learns how to win, they all learn how to win. Every company has this winning DNA buried in their data. Very few have tapped into it (yet).

If you can't show AI impact at every stage of the journey, the budget conversation becomes very uncomfortable very fast. Here's the framework I use to track ROI across the three stages of an AI journey and why the order matters more than the tools. Stage one is foundation. Fix the inputs first: CRM data, tactical automation, follow-up execution. Get that right, and you'll see more selling time per rep, faster follow-up speeds, and CRM completeness that actually holds. Stage two is scale. With a clean foundation underneath, AI starts improving the quality of what comes out: win rates, pipeline coverage, forecast accuracy, rep ramp time. This is where the productivity numbers get interesting. Stage three is new economics. Lower CAC, higher quota attainment, more revenue per rep, stronger NDR. You're not just running a tighter ship, you're reshaping the economics of the entire GTM motion. The sequencing is everything. Teams that try to skip to stage three without the foundation in place end up with expensive AI and the same old results. Done right, this is how you get asymmetric growth—accelerating the top line while the cost base shrinks.

Over the next five years, RevOps is going to become the most strategic function in sales. It comes down to one word: orchestration. As companies deploy more AI agents, someone has to define how humans and agents work together. Who owns what? Where the handoffs are. How the whole system stays aligned. RevOps is already wired for that. They already think across process, the roles and responsibilities, and the rules of engagement in a way that no other function does. Great RevOps leaders will sit down with the CRO, map the underlying motion, and draw a clean line between what humans execute and what agents handle. That's how RevOps will quickly go from cost center to the engine behind asymmetric growth.

Most CROs I talk to are under the same mandate right now: grow the number, don't add headcount. In nearly every sales org I've audited, the capacity to do both is already there. Closers are spending hours on tactical work that has nothing to do with closing: prepping playbooks manually, updating systems, getting coached after the fact instead of in the moment. When AI identifies what your best closers are actually doing, codifies it, and automates it across the rest of the team, reps start covering more accounts. Close rates hold. The math starts working in your favor without a single new hire. One org I worked with put this system in place and saw reps cover 25% more accounts with no drop in close rates. For a 100-person sales team, that's the equivalent of 25 additional reps — headcount that doesn't show up on payroll. The capacity was always there. It was just buried under work that AI should have been doing all along.

RevOps teams spend months building great sales processes. Then nobody uses them. I hear this constantly, and when I dig into why, the answer is almost always the same: reps are the ones being asked to log every interaction, update deal stages, and keep the CRM current after every call. But reps don't have time for that, and that's not how their brains are wired. What fixes it is removing the human from the CRM update loop entirely. When AI pulls data directly from calls, emails, and rep interactions, the sales process stays current without anyone having to maintain it. Deals get scored automatically. Reps see exactly where they're losing points and what it would take to improve.

By the time a stalled deal surfaces in your pipeline review, the roadblock has been sitting in an email thread for weeks. The signals are almost always there, but they're buried in implementation conversations, offhand comments from the buying team, and email threads that reps are expected to manually connect. Terret's agents monitor deal activity across every signal — calls, emails, CRM — and surface critical roadblocks before they stall progress. In this example, the agent flagged an IT freeze, data migration concerns, and a competing internal project, all extracted from an email thread weeks before they became a problem. That's the difference between reacting to a slipped deal and preventing one.

Grow the top line. Cut the bottom line. Do both at the same time. For CROs this tension is real. The problem is that most don't have a reliable way to tell what is working vs. not, fast enough to make the decisions that would give them a fighting chance. AI changes that equation because it can look across the business and identify where growth is actually happening. EMEA on fire? Shift RevOps capacity there, start hiring on that side of the pond. A region's shrinking? Pull resources back. Stop funding what isn't working. The result is a business that's accelerating in the right places and trimming the fat where it can based on real signals rather than quarterly gut calls. This is the third stage of an AI journey. Not just rep productivity. Not just forecast accuracy. The whole economic model changes.

Agentic selling is forcing a new question inside revenue teams: Who decides what humans should do and what agents should do? That decision can't live inside individual tools or one-off automations. It requires someone who understands the full system: Strategy, process, roles, and how work actually gets done. That's where RevOps comes in. Great RevOps leaders will sit down with the CRO, define the strategy, map the underlying process, and then clearly break down what humans should perform and what agents should perform. That's why I believe that over the next five years, RevOps will quickly become the most important function in sales. When RevOps orchestrates humans and agents, the lift is massive. And that's what actually changes the economics of the business.

The more senior you are in an org, the harder it is to get honest feedback. Your team and peers aren't always going to stick their neck out and risk the relationship by being direct. I found a way around that. I've set up AI with a standing instruction: 'Every time I ask for feedback, rate me on a scale of 1 to 10, explain why you're giving me that rating, and tell me what it would have taken to get to a 10.' Then I drop in my meeting transcripts and let it run. One thing to be aware of: you have to explicitly tell AI you want the straight scoop. Because left to its own devices, AI will kiss up to you. You have to expressly instruct it to override that. But when you set it up that way, what comes back is the kind of feedback most executives never get.
